what_it_does: A noticeboard item that assesses Obsidian's Properties / frontmatter UI as a surface for viewing and editing the ledger's YAML frontmatter, concluding it is useful for scalar read-only inspection but risky for authoring because nested list-of-object fields are poorly handled and every edit is a write.
observations: YAML frontmatter carries ledger metadata: id, address, type=block, source=claude_code, state=current, title, author_session, channel, links with a part_of relation, and a history transition.
The body is section 5 of a larger assessment, labeled P8, and evaluates the Properties / frontmatter UI.
Flat scalar frontmatter fields are said to render well in Obsidian's Properties panel.
Nested list-of-object fields such as links: and history: are described as poorly handled by Obsidian's Properties UI and error-prone to edit by hand.
Any edit in the Properties UI is a WRITE and therefore enters the two-writer conflict problem.
The item marks Axis 1 as partial and Axis 2 as red, treating the UI as read-display only rather than an authoring surface.
standouts: Explicit safety ruling: the Properties UI is write-risky and should not be used to author ledger items.
Nested frontmatter structures are identified as the main reason the UI is unsuitable.
Uses a compact two-axis status shorthand: partial / red.
conventions: Follows the ledger noticeboard item schema with YAML frontmatter, id, address, type, source, state, links, and history transitions.
Uses a markdown heading and numbered section format for an assessment note.
Records provenance via author_session and timestamps.
concerns: The file itself warns that editing this kind of file through Obsidian's Properties UI is dangerous, which is a meta-concern about the tooling around the ledger.
The evaluation is qualitative and lacks reproduction steps or concrete failure examples.
No code symbols or implementation details are present.
notes: This item flags Obsidian's Properties panel as a read-only scalar viewer for ledger frontmatter, not an editor, because nested fields and concurrent writes make it unsafe.
questions: What is the two-writer conflict and how is it triggered?
What do Axis 1 and Axis 2 represent?
What is P8 and where do sections 1-4 live?
What alternative UI is intended for editing links and history safely?
purpose_vs_actual: Yes — the title and body both state the file is an assessment of the Properties/frontmatter UI, and the content is exactly that assessment.
intention_signals: The item appears to be trying to steer authors away from using Obsidian's Properties UI for ledger items and to justify why only scalar fields should be viewed there.
novelty: It frames a generic Obsidian feature through a project-specific risk lens, using a compact two-axis status label (partial / red) to encode both capability and danger.
contribution: A documented constraint that the fused system should preserve: Obsidian's Properties UI is unsafe for editing nested ledger frontmatter and should be treated as read-only for scalar fields only.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard assessment item declaring Obsidian's Properties/frontmatter UI useful for read-only scalar inspection but unsafe for authoring due to nested list-of-object fields and write-conflict risk.
intention_for_embedding: To record a tooling guideline that the Properties UI should not be used to edit ledger items.
